schools.summary: The LORD directing the route of Israel: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — God, having brought Israel out (Exodus 12:51), continuing to direct their way (the LORD ordering the route, vv.2, toward the sea, the providence guiding the redeemed, the divine direction of the march); the LORD speaking (directing the journey, vv.2) — the providence (the God who led his people, by the pillar, Exodus 13:21); the LORD directing the route (toward Pi-hahiroth, by the sea, v.2) — the providence (the way that seemed to entangle them, v.3, designed to display God's glory in the deliverance, v.4, the divine ordering of the march) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD directing the route of Israel
God ordering the way of the redeemed (Exodus 13:21)
the route designed to display God's glory (v.4)

schools.summary: The encampment by the sea at God's command: 'speak unto the children of Israel, that they turn and encamp before Pihahiroth, between Migdol and the sea, over against Baalzephon: before it shall ye encamp by the sea' — God directing Israel to turn and encamp by the Red Sea (at Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and the sea, the route that seemed to trap them, the sea before, the mountains and wilderness around, Pharaoh behind, the providence, the position designed to draw Pharaoh on, and display God's deliverance, vv.3-4); the turning (a change of route, toward the sea) — the position that left them apparently trapped (the providence, the seeming danger that God would turn to glory); 'before it shall ye encamp by the sea' (the encampment by the sea, the position appointed by God, the providence, the place where the great deliverance would be wrought, the sea divided, vv.21-22, the divine design, drawing Pharaoh into the trap of his own destruction) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Speak unto the children of Israel, that they turn and encamp before Pihahiroth, between Migdol and the sea... before it shall ye encamp by the sea
the encampment by the sea at God's command
the position that seemed a trap (the sea before, Pharaoh behind)
appointed for the great deliverance, drawing Pharaoh on (vv.3-4, 21-22)

schools.summary: God foreseeing Pharaoh's reading of Israel's plight: 'for Pharaoh will say of the children of Israel, They are entangled in the land, the wilderness hath shut them in' — God foretelling that Pharaoh, seeing Israel's position (boxed in by the sea and wilderness), would think them trapped and helpless (the divine foreknowledge, God knowing the king's thoughts, the bait, the seeming entanglement luring Pharaoh to pursue); Pharaoh's supposed reasoning (Israel trapped, an easy prey) — the king's confidence (the providence, the seeming weakness drawing the enemy to his ruin, the bait set); 'the wilderness hath shut them in' (Pharaoh's view, the wilderness and sea hemming Israel in, no escape, the divine design, the very position that looked like a trap for Israel was a trap for Pharaoh, vv.4, the providence overruling the king's confidence to his destruction) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: For Pharaoh will say of the children of Israel, They are entangled in the land, the wilderness hath shut them in
God foreseeing Pharaoh's reading of Israel's plight
the seeming entanglement luring Pharaoh to pursue
the trap for Israel made a trap for Pharaoh (v.4)

schools.summary: God hardening Pharaoh to be glorified upon him: 'and I will harden Pharaoh's heart, that he shall follow after them; and I will be honoured upon Pharaoh, and upon all his host; that the Egyptians may know that I am the LORD. And they did so' — God would harden Pharaoh's heart once more (the judicial hardening, Exodus 4:21; 7:3, following his persistent self-hardening), so that he would pursue Israel (the king drawn to his destruction, by his own hardened heart, overruled by God, the providence luring the enemy); 'I will be honoured upon Pharaoh' — God would get himself glory (be honored, glorified) upon Pharaoh and his host (by their overthrow in the sea, vv.27-28, the purpose of the hardening, the display of God's power and justice in the destruction of the proud enemy, Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17, 22, the glory of God in the judgment, the overthrow of Pharaoh the manifestation of God's power, the song of triumph, Exodus 15:1-21); 'that the Egyptians may know that I am the LORD. And they did so' (the purpose: that the Egyptians, the survivors, and the world, might know the LORD, his power, by the overthrow, the answer to Pharaoh's 'Who is the LORD?', Exodus 5:2, the knowledge of God by the judgment, and Israel 'did so,' obeyed, encamping as commanded) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And I will harden Pharaoh's heart, that he shall follow after them; and I will be honoured upon Pharaoh, and upon all his host; that the Egyptians may know that I am the LORD
God hardening Pharaoh to be glorified upon him
the proud enemy drawn to his overthrow, the glory of God in the judgment (Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17)
the Egyptians to know the LORD, the answer to 'Who is the LORD?' (Exodus 5:2)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's change of mind and pursuit: 'and it was told the king of Egypt that the people fled: and the heart of Pharaoh and of his servants was turned against the people, and they said, Why have we done this, that we have let Israel go from serving us?' — Pharaoh told that Israel had fled (gone for good, not merely to worship and return), the king's heart, and his servants', turned again against Israel (the regret, repenting of letting them go, the renewed enmity); the turning of heart (regret at losing their slaves) — the renewed hostility (the covetousness and pride reasserting, the loss of their bondmen, the hardening returning); 'Why have we done this, that we have let Israel go from serving us?' (Pharaoh's regret, repenting of the release, grieving the loss of the labor, the worldly sorrow, not for sin, but for the loss, the providence, the regret driving the pursuit, vv.6-9, to the king's destruction) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And it was told the king of Egypt that the people fled: and the heart of Pharaoh and of his servants was turned against the people, and they said, Why have we done this, that we have let Israel go from serving us?
Pharaoh's change of mind and pursuit
the king repenting of the release, grieving the loss of the labor
the regret driving the pursuit to his ruin (vv.6-9)

schools.summary: Pharaoh preparing his chariot and host: 'and he made ready his chariot, and took his people with him' — Pharaoh, his heart turned (v.5), preparing his chariot and gathering his people (his army) for the pursuit (the king himself leading the chase, the mustering of the host, vv.7, the pursuit begun); Pharaoh making ready his own chariot (leading the pursuit in person) — the king's eagerness (the haste of the chase); 'took his people with him' (Pharaoh gathering his army, the people, the host, the chariots, v.7, the mustering for the pursuit, the providence, the host drawn after Israel, to its overthrow in the sea, vv.27-28) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he made ready his chariot, and took his people with him
Pharaoh preparing his chariot and host
the king himself leading the pursuit in person
the host mustered for the chase to its ruin (vv.27-28)

schools.summary: The great army of chariots: 'and he took six hundred chosen chariots, and all the chariots of Egypt, and captains over every one of them' — Pharaoh taking six hundred chosen (picked, elite) chariots, with all the chariots of Egypt, each with its captain (the great army, the full military might of Egypt, the formidable host, the chariots the terror of ancient warfare, the pursuit in force); the great force (six hundred elite chariots, and all the rest, the whole armed might) — the formidable army (the overwhelming force, against the unarmed, fleeing Israel); 'captains over every one of them' (the chariots officered, captains, the disciplined host, the full military array, the providence, the great host gathered, to perish in the sea, vv.27-28, the mightier the enemy, the greater God's glory in the deliverance) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And he took six hundred chosen chariots, and all the chariots of Egypt, and captains over every one of them
the great army of chariots
the full military might of Egypt, the formidable host
gathered to perish in the sea (vv.27-28)

schools.summary: The LORD hardening Pharaoh and Israel's bold going-out: 'and the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and he pursued after the children of Israel: and the children of Israel went out with an high hand' — God hardening Pharaoh (as foretold, v.4), so that he pursued Israel (the judicial hardening, drawing the king to his ruin), and Israel going out 'with an high hand' (boldly, triumphantly, openly, under God's protection, the contrast, the pursuing king, the triumphant people); the LORD hardening (the judicial hardening, v.4; Romans 9:18) — the king drawn on (the providence luring the enemy to destruction); 'the children of Israel went out with an high hand' (Israel going out boldly, with a high hand, openly, triumphantly, not as fugitives but as a delivered people, under God's mighty hand, Numbers 33:3, the triumph of the redeemed, the providence, the bold going-out under God's protection) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and he pursued after the children of Israel: and the children of Israel went out with an high hand
the LORD hardening Pharaoh and Israel's bold going-out
the king drawn to his ruin (v.4; Romans 9:18)
Israel triumphant under God's mighty hand (Numbers 33:3)

schools.summary: The Egyptians overtaking Israel by the sea: 'but the Egyptians pursued after them, all the horses and chariots of Pharaoh, and his horsemen, and his army, and overtook them encamping by the sea, beside Pihahiroth, before Baalzephon' — the full Egyptian army (horses, chariots, horsemen) pursuing and overtaking Israel encamped by the sea (at Pi-hahiroth, the host catching up with the seemingly trapped people, the crisis, the enemy behind, the sea before, vv.10-12, the providence, the position appointed for the deliverance); the whole army (horses, chariots, horsemen) — the formidable pursuit (the overwhelming force); 'overtook them encamping by the sea, beside Pihahiroth' (the army overtaking Israel, boxed in by the sea, vv.2-3, the crisis, the people trapped, to all appearance, the providence, the sea before, the enemy behind, the place of the great deliverance, vv.21-22) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: But the Egyptians pursued after them... and overtook them encamping by the sea, beside Pihahiroth, before Baalzephon
the Egyptians overtaking Israel by the sea
the enemy behind, the sea before, the people seemingly trapped
the place appointed for the great deliverance (vv.21-22)

schools.summary: Israel's terror and cry at the approach of Egypt: 'and when Pharaoh drew nigh, the children of Israel lifted up their eyes, and, behold, the Egyptians marched after them; and they were sore afraid: and the children of Israel cried out unto the LORD' — Israel, seeing the Egyptian army advancing (the sea before, the enemy behind), greatly afraid (the terror of the trapped people, no escape, to all appearance, the failing of faith, the people overwhelmed by the danger); the great fear (the people, lately triumphant, v.8, now terrified) — the weakness of faith under the crisis (the danger overwhelming the recent deliverance, the trembling of the redeemed); 'the children of Israel cried out unto the LORD' (the people crying to the LORD, in their fear, a cry of distress, mingled, the witnesses note, with some faith and much fear, even with murmuring against Moses, vv.11-12, the cry of the trapped, the right resort, to God, though in weakness, the providence, the cry that God would answer, vv.13-14) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And when Pharaoh drew nigh, the children of Israel lifted up their eyes... they were sore afraid: and the children of Israel cried out unto the LORD
Israel's terror and cry at the approach of Egypt
the failing of faith under the crisis (the sea before, the enemy behind)
the cry to the LORD, the right resort though in weakness (vv.13-14)

schools.summary: Israel's murmuring against Moses: 'and they said unto Moses, Because there were no graves in Egypt, hast thou taken us away to die in the wilderness? wherefore hast thou dealt thus with us, to carry us forth out of Egypt?' — Israel, in their terror, murmuring bitterly (even sarcastically) against Moses, blaming him for bringing them out to die (the murmuring of unbelief, the people turning on the deliverer, the bitter reproach, the sarcasm about graves, ironic in the land of tombs); the bitter complaint (blaming Moses for the danger) — the murmuring of fear and unbelief (the people forgetting the late wonders, turning on the deliverer, the weakness of faith); 'to carry us forth out of Egypt' (the reproach, as if the deliverance were a calamity, the ingratitude, the people preferring bondage to the trial of faith, vv.12, the providence, the murmuring that God's deliverance would rebuke, vv.13-14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, JFB.

themes: And they said unto Moses, Because there were no graves in Egypt, hast thou taken us away to die in the wilderness?...
Israel's murmuring against Moses
the murmuring of fear and unbelief, the people turning on the deliverer
the ingratitude, preferring bondage to the trial of faith (v.12)

schools.summary: Israel preferring bondage to the trial: 'is not this the word that we did tell thee in Egypt, saying, Let us alone, that we may serve the Egyptians? For it had been better for us to serve the Egyptians, than that we should die in the wilderness' — Israel claiming they had said in Egypt (Exodus 5:21) to be left alone, to serve the Egyptians, rather than face this (preferring bondage to the trial of faith, the murmuring, the cowardly preference of slavery over the risk of freedom, the failing of faith); the people's wish (to serve Egypt rather than die) — the cowardice of unbelief (the preference of bondage, the failing faith); 'better for us to serve the Egyptians' (the ingratitude, preferring slavery to God's deliverance, the murmuring of fear, the people forgetting God's power and promise, the providence, the unbelief rebuked by the deliverance, vv.13-14) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Is not this the word that we did tell thee in Egypt... Let us alone, that we may serve the Egyptians? For it had been better for us to serve the Egyptians, than that we should die in the wilderness
Israel preferring bondage to the trial
the cowardly preference of slavery over the risk of freedom (Exodus 5:21)
the murmuring of unbelief, forgetting God's power and promise

schools.summary: Moses' word of faith - 'stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD': 'and Moses said unto the people, Fear ye not, stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD, which he will shew to you to day: for the Egyptians whom ye have seen to day, ye shall see them again no more for ever' — the great word — 'Fear ye not, stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD, which he will shew to you to day': Moses, against the people's terror, calling them to faith — 'fear not' (the courage of faith), 'stand still' (cease your panic and murmuring, do not flee, wait on God), and 'see the salvation of the LORD' (behold the deliverance God himself would work, the call to faith and patience, the deliverance to be God's own work, not man's, the people to stand still and let God save, Psalm 46:10, 'Be still, and know that I am God,' Isaiah 30:15, 'in quietness and in confidence shall be your strength,' the lesson — in extremity, when all means fail, faith stands still and waits on God's salvation, the salvation wholly of the LORD, the redeemed only to behold it, the great word of faith for every trial, 2 Chronicles 20:17, 'ye shall not need to fight... stand ye still, and see the salvation of the LORD'); 'for the Egyptians whom ye have seen to day, ye shall see them again no more for ever' (the promise, the Egyptians, so dreaded, would be seen no more, being destroyed in the sea, vv.27-28, the assurance of the complete deliverance, the enemy to be utterly removed, the salvation of God); Moses' word (fear not, stand still, see) — the call to faith in extremity (the salvation of the LORD, the deliverance wholly God's, the providence, the great deliverance about to be wrought, vv.21-22) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD' call to?

sides.position: The call to faith and patience in extremity - to cease the panic and wait on God, the deliverance to be God's own work alone (Psalm 46:10; Isaiah 30:15; 2 Chronicles 20:17); the great word of faith for every trial, when all means fail, to stand still and behold the salvation of God

themes: And Moses said unto the people, Fear ye not, stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD, which he will shew to you to day...
Moses' word of faith - 'stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD' (the crux of the call to faith)
the call to faith and patience in extremity, the deliverance wholly God's work (Psalm 46:10; Isaiah 30:15)
the great word of faith for every trial, when all means fail (2 Chronicles 20:17)

schools.summary: The LORD fighting for Israel while they hold their peace: 'the LORD shall fight for you, and ye shall hold your peace' — Moses assuring the people that the LORD himself would fight for them (the deliverance his own work, by his power, vv.24-28), while they need only 'hold their peace' (be still, silent, trusting, the LORD the warrior, fighting for his people, Exodus 15:3, 'the LORD is a man of war,' the rest of faith, the people to be still and let God work); the LORD fighting (the deliverance God's own battle, Deuteronomy 1:30; 3:22; 20:4, 'the LORD your God... fighteth for you') — the divine warrior (the salvation by God's power alone); 'ye shall hold your peace' (the people to be silent, still, trusting, ceasing the murmuring and panic, v.10-12, the rest of faith, the redeemed only to trust and behold, while God fights, the lesson — in God's deliverances, the believer's part is often to be still and trust, the salvation wholly of God, Isaiah 30:15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: The LORD shall fight for you, and ye shall hold your peace
the LORD fighting for Israel while they hold their peace
the divine warrior fighting his people's battle (Deuteronomy 1:30; Exodus 15:3)
the rest of faith - the redeemed to be still and trust (Isaiah 30:15)

schools.summary: God's command to go forward: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Wherefore criest thou unto me? speak unto the children of Israel, that they go forward' — God's word to Moses (who was crying to the LORD, perhaps silently, in the crisis): 'wherefore criest thou unto me?' — not a rebuke of prayer, but a call to action (the time for prayer now also the time for the forward step, the providence, God bidding Moses lead the people on, the call from crying to acting); 'speak unto the children of Israel, that they go forward' — God commanding Israel to 'go forward' — to advance toward the sea (which would divide before them, v.16, the command of faith, to move forward, trusting God to make the way, the lesson — faith not only stands still to see God's salvation, v.13, but also goes forward at God's command, even toward the sea, trusting God to open the way, the obedience of faith, stepping out on God's word, Hebrews 11:29, 'by faith they passed through the Red sea,' the venture of faith, advancing where no way appears, trusting God to make one); 'go forward' (the call to advance, the time for prayer now the time for the step of faith, the providence, the forward movement that God would honor by dividing the sea, v.16, 21-22) — Henry, Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does 'that they go forward' teach?

sides.position: The command of faith to advance toward the sea, trusting God to make the way (Hebrews 11:29) - faith not only standing still (v.13) but stepping forward on God's word, the venture of faith advancing where no way appears; 'wherefore criest thou?' the call from prayer to obedient action

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Wherefore criest thou unto me? speak unto the children of Israel, that they go forward
God's command to go forward
the command of faith to advance toward the sea, trusting God to make the way (Hebrews 11:29)
the call from prayer to obedient action, the venture of faith where no way appears

schools.summary: The command to divide the sea: 'but lift thou up thy rod, and stretch out thine hand over the sea, and divide it: and the children of Israel shall go on dry ground through the midst of the sea' — God commanding Moses to lift the rod (the instrument of the wonders) and stretch his hand over the sea to divide it, that Israel might pass through on dry ground (the great miracle commanded, the sea to part, the way of deliverance, through the sea, the rod the instrument, as the plagues, Exodus 7:19); the dividing of the sea (by God's power, through Moses' rod) — the great wonder (the way opened through the deep); 'the children of Israel shall go on dry ground through the midst of the sea' (Israel to pass through the divided sea on dry ground, vv.22, 29, the deliverance through the sea, a type of baptism, 1 Corinthians 10:1-2, 'baptized unto Moses in the cloud and in the sea,' the way of salvation through the deep, the providence) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But lift thou up thy rod, and stretch out thine hand over the sea, and divide it: and the children of Israel shall go on dry ground through the midst of the sea
the command to divide the sea
the great miracle, the way of deliverance through the deep
a type of baptism (1 Corinthians 10:1-2)

schools.summary: God hardening Egypt to follow into the sea: 'and I, behold, I will harden the hearts of the Egyptians, and they shall follow them: and I will get me honour upon Pharaoh, and upon all his host, upon his chariots, and upon his horsemen' — God would harden the Egyptians, so that they would follow Israel into the sea (to their destruction, vv.23, 27-28, the judicial hardening, drawing the enemy into the trap, the providence, the enemy lured to its overthrow, that God might be glorified, v.4); the Egyptians following into the sea (hardened, pursuing recklessly) — the enemy drawn on (the trap); 'I will get me honour upon Pharaoh, and upon all his host, upon his chariots, and upon his horsemen' (God glorified in the overthrow of Pharaoh and his host, the destruction in the sea, vv.27-28, the glory of God in the judgment, v.4; Exodus 15:1-21, the manifestation of God's power, the providence, the enemy's destruction the glory of God) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And I, behold, I will harden the hearts of the Egyptians, and they shall follow them: and I will get me honour upon Pharaoh, and upon all his host
God hardening Egypt to follow into the sea
the enemy lured into the trap to its overthrow (v.4; vv.27-28)
the glory of God in the destruction of the proud host

schools.summary: The Egyptians to know the LORD by the judgment: 'and the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I have gotten me honour upon Pharaoh, upon his chariots, and upon his horsemen' — God's purpose: that the Egyptians (the survivors, and the world) might know the LORD (his power, justice, sovereignty) by the overthrow of Pharaoh and his host (the answer to Pharaoh's 'Who is the LORD?', Exodus 5:2, the knowledge of God by the judgment, the destruction the proof of God's power, vv.27-28); the purpose (the Egyptians to know the LORD) — the answer to the king's defiance (Exodus 5:2, the knowledge of God by the judgment); 'when I have gotten me honour upon Pharaoh, upon his chariots, and upon his horsemen' (God glorified in the overthrow, the destruction the manifestation of his power, vv.27-28, the providence, the judgment teaching the knowledge of God, v.4; Exodus 9:16) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I have gotten me honour upon Pharaoh, upon his chariots, and upon his horsemen
the Egyptians to know the LORD by the judgment
the knowledge of God by the overthrow, the answer to 'Who is the LORD?' (Exodus 5:2)
the destruction the proof of God's power (vv.27-28)

schools.summary: The angel and the pillar moving between Israel and Egypt: 'and the angel of God, which went before the camp of Israel, removed and went behind them; and the pillar of the cloud went from before their face, and stood behind them' — the angel of God (the Angel of the LORD, in the pillar, Exodus 13:21; 23:20-21, whom many identify as the pre-incarnate Christ, the Angel of the covenant) who had gone before Israel, now removing and going behind them (between the camps, the divine presence interposing, the providence guarding his people); the Angel of God (in the pillar, the divine presence, the Angel of the covenant, Exodus 23:20-21) — God himself going before and now behind (the guardian presence); 'the pillar of the cloud went from before their face, and stood behind them' (the pillar, the visible token of God's presence, moving to stand between Israel and the Egyptians, v.20, the divine interposition, God himself coming between his people and the enemy, the providence, the guard, the shield of the redeemed, the LORD a wall between them and their foe) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the angel of God, which went before the camp of Israel, removed and went behind them; and the pillar of the cloud went from before their face, and stood behind them
the angel and the pillar moving between Israel and Egypt
the Angel of the covenant (Exodus 23:20-21), the divine presence interposing
God himself coming between his people and the enemy, the guard of the redeemed

schools.summary: The cloud dark to Egypt but light to Israel: 'and it came between the camp of the Egyptians and the camp of Israel; and it was a cloud and darkness to them, but it gave light by night to these: so that the one came not near the other all the night' — the pillar coming between the two camps, being darkness to the Egyptians (hiding Israel, hindering the pursuit) but light to Israel (lighting their way, the divine interposition, the cloud a barrier and a guide, the providence, the distinction, light to God's people, darkness to the enemy, as the plague of darkness, Exodus 10:23); the double effect (darkness to Egypt, light to Israel) — the distinction of grace (the same cloud, opposite effects, as the gospel, a savor of death to some, of life to others, 2 Corinthians 2:15-16, the divine guard and guide); 'the one came not near the other all the night' (the cloud keeping the camps apart all night, the enemy held back, the divine barrier, protecting Israel while they crossed, vv.21-22, the providence, God a wall between his people and their foe) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And it came between the camp of the Egyptians and the camp of Israel; and it was a cloud and darkness to them, but it gave light by night to these
the cloud dark to Egypt but light to Israel
the distinction of grace - light to God's people, darkness to the enemy (Exodus 10:23; 2 Corinthians 2:15-16)
the divine barrier keeping the camps apart all night

schools.summary: The dividing of the Red Sea: 'and Moses stretched out his hand over the sea; and the LORD caused the sea to go back by a strong east wind all that night, and made the sea dry land, and the waters were divided' — Moses stretching out his hand (with the rod, v.16), and the LORD dividing the sea, driving it back by a strong east wind all night, making the sea-bed dry land (the great miracle, the sea parted, a way through the deep, the means, the east wind, the natural agent in God's hand, yet the dividing supernatural, the waters standing as walls, v.22); the LORD (not the wind alone) dividing the sea — the east wind the instrument, but the standing of the waters as walls (v.22), the dry ground, the timing, all supernatural (the witnesses rejecting the naturalistic explaining-away, a mere wind could not heap the waters as walls and dry the bed in a night, then return to drown the host, vv.27-28, the miracle of God's power, the great deliverance, Psalm 78:13; 106:9; Isaiah 63:12-13; the LORD dividing the sea); 'made the sea dry land, and the waters were divided' (the sea-bed dry, the waters divided, a way through the midst, vv.22, the deliverance through the sea, the type of baptism, 1 Corinthians 10:1-2, the passage through the deep, Hebrews 11:29, the providence, the great salvation, the way where no way was) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: Was the dividing of the sea a mere natural effect of the east wind?

sides.position: No - the east wind was the instrument, but the dividing was supernatural (the waters standing as walls, v.22, the dry bed, the timing); a mere wind could not heap the waters as walls and dry the bed in a night, then return to drown the host (vv.27-28); the great miracle of God's power (Psalm 78:13; Isaiah 63:12-13)

themes: And Moses stretched out his hand over the sea; and the LORD caused the sea to go back by a strong east wind all that night, and made the sea dry land, and the waters were divided
the dividing of the Red Sea (the crux of the great deliverance)
the east wind the instrument, but the dividing supernatural (the waters as walls, v.22)
the great miracle of God's power, the way of salvation through the deep (Psalm 78:13; 1 Corinthians 10:1-2; Hebrews 11:29)

schools.summary: Israel passing through the sea on dry ground: 'and the children of Israel went into the midst of the sea upon the dry ground: and the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left' — Israel passing through the divided sea on dry ground (the sea-bed dried, v.21, the deliverance through the deep, the way opened by God's power, the obedience of faith, the people going in, trusting God, Hebrews 11:29, 'by faith they passed through the Red sea as by dry land,' the great passage); the dry ground (the sea-bed made dry, no mire) — the complete miracle (not only the waters parted, but the ground dried, the safe passage); 'the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left' (the divided waters standing as walls on either side, a protection and a wonder, the waters held back by God's power, the miracle, the waters as walls, not a mere ebb-tide, but the deep heaped up, the providence, the way through the sea, walled by the waters, the type of baptism, 1 Corinthians 10:1-2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the children of Israel went into the midst of the sea upon the dry ground: and the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left
Israel passing through the sea on dry ground
the obedience of faith, passing through the deep (Hebrews 11:29)
the waters a wall, the way walled by the deep, a type of baptism (1 Corinthians 10:1-2)

schools.summary: The Egyptians pursuing into the sea: 'and the Egyptians pursued, and went in after them to the midst of the sea, even all Pharaoh's horses, his chariots, and his horsemen' — the Egyptians (hardened, v.17), seeing Israel cross, recklessly pursuing into the midst of the sea (the whole host, horses, chariots, horsemen, the enemy drawn into the trap, the way that saved Israel becoming the snare of Egypt, the providence, the host lured to its destruction, vv.27-28); the reckless pursuit (the hardened Egyptians following into the divided sea, blind to the danger) — the obstinacy (the enemy drawn on by God's hardening, v.17); 'all Pharaoh's horses, his chariots, and his horsemen' (the whole army in the sea, the entire host pursuing, the trap, the host now in the midst, to be drowned, vv.27-28, the providence, the enemy gathered for destruction) — JFB, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the Egyptians pursued, and went in after them to the midst of the sea, even all Pharaoh's horses, his chariots, and his horsemen
the Egyptians pursuing into the sea
the hardened host recklessly following, drawn into the trap (v.17)
the enemy lured to its destruction (vv.27-28)

schools.summary: The LORD troubling the Egyptian host: 'and it came to pass, that in the morning watch the LORD looked unto the host of the Egyptians through the pillar of fire and of the cloud, and troubled the host of the Egyptians' — in the morning watch (the last hours of night), the LORD 'looking' (from the pillar of fire and cloud) upon the Egyptian host and troubling (confounding, throwing into panic) them (the divine intervention, God himself confounding the enemy, the providence, the host in the midst of the sea thrown into terror and disorder); the LORD's looking (from the pillar, the manifestation of his presence and power, perhaps with thunder, lightning, storm, Psalm 77:16-18) — the divine terror (the presence that confounded the enemy); 'troubled the host of the Egyptians' (the host thrown into confusion and panic, by God's intervention, the storm, the terror, the disorder, the chariots clogged, v.25, the providence, the enemy confounded in the midst of the sea, the deliverance and the judgment) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, that in the morning watch the LORD looked unto the host of the Egyptians through the pillar of fire and of the cloud, and troubled the host of the Egyptians
the LORD troubling the Egyptian host
God himself looking from the pillar and confounding the enemy (Psalm 77:16-18)
the host thrown into panic in the midst of the sea

schools.summary: The Egyptians' panic and flight too late: 'and took off their chariot wheels, that they drave them heavily: so that the Egyptians said, Let us flee from the face of Israel; for the LORD fighteth for them against the Egyptians' — God clogging the chariot wheels (binding them, or making them stick in the soft sea-bed), so that the Egyptians drove with difficulty (the divine hindering, the host bogged down, helpless, in the midst of the sea, the providence, the enemy disabled); the Egyptians' terror (now trying to flee, too late) — the panic (the recognition of the danger, the flight attempted); 'for the LORD fighteth for them against the Egyptians' (the Egyptians' forced confession, the LORD fighting for Israel, as Moses had said, v.14, 'the LORD shall fight for you,' the enemy owning God's hand, too late, in their destruction, the providence, the LORD the warrior, Exodus 15:3, confounding the foe, the confession wrung from the dying enemy) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And took off their chariot wheels, that they drave them heavily: so that the Egyptians said, Let us flee from the face of Israel; for the LORD fighteth for them against the Egyptians
the Egyptians' panic and flight too late
the enemy disabled, the chariots clogged in the midst of the sea
the forced confession 'the LORD fighteth for Israel' (v.14; Exodus 15:3)

schools.summary: The command to return the sea upon Egypt: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the sea, that the waters may come again upon the Egyptians, upon their chariots, and upon their horsemen' — God commanding Moses to stretch his hand again over the sea, that the divided waters might return upon the Egyptians (now in the midst, v.23, the command for the judgment, the sea to close upon the enemy, the destruction of the host, the same waters that walled Israel now drowning Egypt); the waters returning (upon the Egyptians, by God's power, through Moses' rod) — the judgment on the enemy (the destruction of the host); 'upon their chariots, and upon their horsemen' (the waters upon the whole host, chariots, horsemen, the complete destruction, the entire army drowned, vv.27-28, the providence, the same sea Israel's deliverance and Egypt's grave) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the sea, that the waters may come again upon the Egyptians, upon their chariots, and upon their horsemen
the command to return the sea upon Egypt
the sea to close upon the enemy in judgment
the same waters Israel's deliverance and Egypt's grave (vv.27-28)

schools.summary: The sea returning and the Egyptians overthrown: 'and Moses stretched forth his hand over the sea, and the sea returned to his strength when the morning appeared; and the Egyptians fled against it; and the LORD overthrew the Egyptians in the midst of the sea' — Moses stretching out his hand, and the sea returning to its full strength (the waters rushing back) at dawn (the judgment executed, the sea closing upon the enemy, the timing, the morning, the host in the midst, the destruction begun); the Egyptians fleeing against the returning waters (fleeing toward the shore, but the waters rushing to meet them) — the futile flight (the enemy caught in the closing sea); 'the LORD overthrew the Egyptians in the midst of the sea' (the LORD, not Moses, not the sea alone, overthrowing, overturning, casting down, the Egyptians in the midst of the sea, the destruction his own act, vv.30-31, the judgment, the host overthrown, drowned, vv.28; Exodus 15:4-5, 'Pharaoh's chariots and his host hath he cast into the sea,' the providence, the enemy destroyed, the deliverance complete) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Moses stretched forth his hand over the sea, and the sea returned to his strength when the morning appeared; and the Egyptians fled against it; and the LORD overthrew the Egyptians in the midst of the sea
the sea returning and the Egyptians overthrown
the host caught in the closing sea, the futile flight
the LORD himself overthrowing the enemy (Exodus 15:4-5)

schools.summary: The total destruction of Pharaoh's host: 'and the waters returned, and covered the chariots, and the horsemen, and all the host of Pharaoh that came into the sea after them; there remained not so much as one of them' — the total overthrow — 'the waters returned, and covered the chariots, and the horsemen, and all the host of Pharaoh... there remained not so much as one of them': the returning waters covering (drowning) the chariots, horsemen, and the whole host of Pharaoh that had pursued into the sea (the complete destruction, the entire army drowned, not one surviving, the judgment on the proud enemy, the host that defied God and oppressed his people, now perished, Exodus 15:4-10, 'the depths have covered them: they sank into the bottom as a stone,' the glory of God in the overthrow, v.4, 18); the whole pursuing host destroyed (all that came into the sea) — the complete judgment (the enemy utterly overthrown); 'there remained not so much as one of them' (not one of the pursuing host surviving, the complete destruction, whether Pharaoh himself perished the witnesses debate, but his host was utterly destroyed, Psalm 136:15, 'overthrew Pharaoh and his host in the Red sea,' the total judgment, the providence, the enemy of God's people destroyed, the deliverance complete, the type of the final overthrow of all God's enemies, Revelation 19:21, the destruction of the strong man, Hebrews 2:14-15) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill, Clarke.

debates.question: What does the drowning of the host signify?

sides.position: The total destruction of the pursuing host (chariots, horsemen, all Pharaoh's army drowned, not one remaining, Exodus 15:4-10; Psalm 136:15) - the judgment on the proud enemy that defied God and oppressed his people, the glory of God in the overthrow, a type of the final overthrow of God's enemies and the destruction of the strong man (Hebrews 2:14-15)

themes: And the waters returned, and covered the chariots, and the horsemen, and all the host of Pharaoh that came into the sea after them; there remained not so much as one of them
the total destruction of Pharaoh's host (the crux of the judgment)
the entire pursuing army drowned, not one remaining (Exodus 15:4-10; Psalm 136:15)
the judgment on the proud enemy, a type of the final overthrow of God's enemies (Hebrews 2:14-15)

schools.summary: Israel saved on dry land through the sea: 'but the children of Israel walked upon dry land in the midst of the sea; and the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left' — in contrast to the drowned Egyptians (v.28), Israel walking safely on dry land through the midst of the sea, the waters a wall on either side (the deliverance complete, the same sea Israel's salvation and Egypt's grave, the contrast, the redeemed saved, the enemy destroyed, by the same waters); Israel's safe passage (on dry land, vv.22) — the deliverance (the redeemed kept while the enemy perished); 'the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left' (the same waters a wall for Israel, a grave for Egypt, the distinction of grace, the salvation and the judgment in the one sea, 2 Corinthians 2:15-16, the providence, the redeemed delivered, the enemy destroyed, the great deliverance, Hebrews 11:29) — JFB, Gill.

themes: But the children of Israel walked upon dry land in the midst of the sea; and the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left
Israel saved on dry land through the sea
the redeemed saved while the enemy perished, by the same waters
the same sea salvation and judgment (2 Corinthians 2:15-16; Hebrews 11:29)

schools.summary: The LORD's salvation of Israel and the dead Egyptians: 'thus the LORD saved Israel that day out of the hand of the Egyptians; and Israel saw the Egyptians dead upon the sea shore' — the LORD (not Moses, not Israel) saving his people that day from the Egyptians (the great salvation, the deliverance God's own work, as Moses had said, 'see the salvation of the LORD,' v.13, the redemption complete, Israel saved, the enemy destroyed, the LORD the saviour); 'the LORD saved' (the salvation his own act) — God the deliverer (the redemption his work alone, Psalm 106:8-10, 'he saved them for his name's sake... and saved them from the hand of him that hated them'); 'Israel saw the Egyptians dead upon the sea shore' (the bodies of the Egyptians washed up on the shore, the proof of the deliverance, the enemy visibly destroyed, the sight that confirmed the salvation, the dread enemy now dead, the providence, the deliverance complete, the people seeing the salvation of the LORD, v.13) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Thus the LORD saved Israel that day out of the hand of the Egyptians; and Israel saw the Egyptians dead upon the sea shore
the LORD's salvation of Israel and the dead Egyptians
the salvation God's own work (v.13; Psalm 106:8-10)
the enemy visibly destroyed, the proof of the deliverance

schools.summary: The faith of the redeemed - they feared and believed the LORD: 'and Israel saw that great work which the LORD did upon the Egyptians: and the people feared the LORD, and believed the LORD, and his servant Moses' — Israel seeing the great work (Hebrew, great hand) of the LORD in the overthrow of Egypt (the deliverance witnessed, the mighty hand of God displayed, the great salvation seen); the response — 'the people feared the LORD, and believed the LORD': the people, seeing the great deliverance, fearing the LORD (a reverent awe of his power and majesty) and believing the LORD (trusting him as the true and only God, faithful to his word, the fruit of the deliverance, the fear and faith of the redeemed, the witnesses noting both true effects — the holy fear, reverence, awe of God, and the faith, trust, belief in God and his word, yet some noting the faith was, in many, but a temporary impression, soon to fail in the murmurings, Exodus 15:24; 16:2; Psalm 106:12-13, 'then believed they his words... they soon forgat his works,' the right response in itself, fear and faith, the abiding lesson, the deliverance works the fear and faith of God's people); 'and his servant Moses' (the people believing Moses, as the LORD's servant and prophet, his mission confirmed by the deliverance, the confirmation of Moses' authority, the people now trusting him as God's appointed leader, the deliverance the seal of his commission, the providence, the deliverance establishing the leader, a type of Christ, the greater Deliverer, whose redemption seals his mission) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does the people's fear and faith display?

sides.position: The fear (reverent awe) and faith (trust) wrought by the great deliverance, the right response of the redeemed - though in many but a passing impression (soon failing, Exodus 15:24; Psalm 106:12-13); the abiding lesson that God's mighty works should work the fear and faith of his people, and Moses confirmed as God's servant (a type of Christ the Deliverer)

themes: And Israel saw that great work which the LORD did upon the Egyptians: and the people feared the LORD, and believed the LORD, and his servant Moses
the faith of the redeemed - they feared and believed the LORD (the crux of the deliverance's fruit)
the fear (reverent awe) and faith (trust) wrought by the great deliverance (Psalm 106:12)
Moses confirmed as God's servant, the deliverance the seal of his mission (a type of Christ the Deliverer)
